title: GST Rate Rationalization and Relief Measures
focus: Changes to the GST slab system and specific rate reductions for essential sectors.
rationale: The shift to a two-slab system and specific exemptions, particularly for health insurance and lifesaving drugs, are highly testable facts for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Two-slab system of 5% and 18%, removing the earlier 12% and 28% rate. A 40% Special De-Merit Rate applies to 'sin' goods and select luxuries, replacing the compensation cess levy. The median tax rate effectively moves from 12% to 5%. Relief for Citizens and Key Sectors: Health (18% to Exempt/5%): GST is exempted on all individual life and health insurance policies and their reinsurance.
evidenceBullets: A new two-slab system of 5% and 18% replaces the earlier 12% and 28% rates.
A 40% Special De-Merit Rate applies to 'sin' goods, replacing the compensation cess.
GST is now exempted on all individual life and health insurance policies.
Cement, air conditioners, and all TV sets have dropped from the 28% to the 18% slab.

title: GST Administrative and Export Reforms
focus: Institutional and procedural changes including GSTAT, registration ease, and export benefits.
rationale: Institutional bodies like GSTAT and technical definitions like 'place of supply' for intermediary services are standard UPSC targets.
sectionExcerpt: Dispute Resolution: The Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) is set to be operational for appeals before the end of September 2025. Registration Ease: A simplified, optional GST registration scheme is recommended for small and low-risk businesses. Export Benefits: Recommended that the place of supply for 'intermediary services' will be determined as per the location of the recipient of such services.
evidenceBullets: The Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) will be operational by September 2025.
A simplified optional GST registration scheme for small businesses will be operational from November 2025.
The place of supply for intermediary services is now determined by the location of the recipient, aiding Indian exporters.

title: GST Council
focus: Constitutional provisions, composition, and voting mechanism of the GST Council.
rationale: The GST Council's constitutional basis (Article 279A) and unique voting weightage are classic polity and economy crossover questions frequently asked in UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: Constitutional Basis: Established under Article 279A, the apex decision-making body on GST matters. Composition: Chaired by the Union Finance Minister, with the Union MoS (Revenue/Finance) and Finance Ministers of all states/UTs with legislatures as members. Voting: Uses a weighted system; Centre has one-third vote, states have two-thirds; decisions need three-fourths majority of weighted votes.
evidenceBullets: The GST Council is a constitutional body established under Article 279A.
It is chaired by the Union Finance Minister.
The Centre holds one-third of the voting weight, while states hold two-thirds.
Decisions in the GST Council require a three-fourths majority of weighted votes.
